medications.indication_rationale: Liberal emollients reduce scaling and itch and are the mainstay of comfort for guttate psoriasis.
A mild topical corticosteroid can reduce itch and inflammation for localised discomfort.
No allergic reaction is present; anaphylaxis treatment is not indicated (retained only in the safety-net if features develop).

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Use lukewarm (not hot) showers, moisturise within 3 minutes of drying, and monitor for the safety-net features.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Reduces scaling/itch and enables escalation if red flags appear.

patient_education_points.point: This is guttate psoriasis — your immune system reacting to the recent strep throat, not a penicillin allergy and not contagious. It usually fades over several weeks.
Moisturise generously and you can use a mild steroid cream for the itch; avoid hot showers. See your GP within 1-2 weeks to confirm and discuss ongoing care.
Go to ED or call 000 immediately if you develop lip/tongue/face swelling, wheeze or trouble breathing, or if the rash becomes non-blanching (doesn't fade under a glass) with a fever.

follow_up_plan.what_to_reassess: Confirm diagnosis, assess extent, consider dermatology/phototherapy referral.
follow_up_plan.trigger_for_earlier_review: Anaphylaxis features, or a non-blanching rash with fever — ED/000.

must_include_items: Screen anaphylaxis and exclude a non-blanching febrile rash before reassuring
Explain guttate psoriasis is post-strep, not a penicillin allergy, and not contagious
Emollient ± mild topical steroid and routine GP/dermatology follow-up
errors_of_commission: Labelling a penicillin allergy and documenting it lifelong
Escalating a well patient with a blanching rash to ED
Prescribing antihistamines/adrenaline for a non-allergic rash
scoring_rubric.passing_threshold_notes: T1 case: passing requires anaphylaxis/meningococcal screen + reassurance (not a drug allergy) + safety-net. Applying a penicillin-allergy label or ED over-triage are failures.
